previous_headings: Using marea to develop FSAR plots
title: Fisheries Science Advisory Report (FSAR) four panel plot example in marea
text: Fisheries Science Advisory Reports (FSAR) provided Canadian Science Advisory Secretariat help decision makers understand information particular fish stock stock assessments. Stock assessments scientific process analyzing available data evaluate abundance, productivity, options harvest levels fish stocks past, present, future.

previous_headings: Using marea to develop FSAR plots
title: Fisheries Science Advisory Report (FSAR) four panel plot example in marea
text: create example FSAR data:
code: library(tidyverse) library(marea)  set.seed(42)  years <- 1970:2025  # Catch: Range ~ 800 to 4200 catch_value <- round(runif(length(years), 800, 4200), 1)  # Biomass: Values rising slowly over years, with uncertainty biomass_value <- round(seq(12, 55, length.out = length(years)) + rnorm(length(years), 0, 3), 1) biomass_lower <- round(biomass_value - runif(length(years), 1.2, 6), 1) biomass_upper <- round(biomass_value + runif(length(years), 1.3, 8), 1)  # Fishing: Rates around 0.06–0.27, with some noise fishing_value <- round(runif(length(years), 0.06, 0.27) + rnorm(length(years), 0, 0.01), 3) fishing_lower <- round(fishing_value - runif(length(years), 0.005, 0.02), 3) fishing_upper <- round(fishing_value + runif(length(years), 0.005, 0.02), 3)  # Recruitment: Generally <1 to ~5, with years of high recruitment and uncertainty recruitment_value <- round(runif(length(years), 0.8, 5.3) + rnorm(length(years), 0, 0.5), 3) recruitment_lower <- round(recruitment_value - runif(length(years), 0.08, 0.5), 3) recruitment_upper <- round(recruitment_value + runif(length(years), 0.08, 0.5), 3)  fsar_fourplot_exdata <- data.frame(   panel.category = rep(c("Catch", "Biomass", "Fishing", "Recruitment"), each = length(years)),   year = rep(years, 4),   ts.name = c(     rep("CanadaTotal", length(years)),     rep("Survey", length(years)),     rep("Ut", length(years)),     rep("RVpred", length(years))   ),   value = c(catch_value, biomass_value, fishing_value, recruitment_value),   lower = c(     rep(NA, length(years)),     biomass_lower,     fishing_lower,     recruitment_lower   ),   upper = c(     rep(NA, length(years)),     biomass_upper,     fishing_upper,     recruitment_upper   ) )

previous_headings: Using marea to develop FSAR plots
title: Fisheries Science Advisory Report (FSAR) four panel plot example in marea
text: Using ea_data allows storage rich metadata alongside time series dataset. Required metadata include: x (data), value_col, data_type, location_descriptor, region, units, source citation. However, can add additional metadata desired.
code: fsar_example <- as_ea_data(   x = fsar_fourplot_exdata,   value_col = "value",   data_type = "stock",   location_descriptor = "NAFO divisions",   region = "Maritimes",   units = "variable",   source_citation = "DFO FSAR 2025 citation",   # additional metadata   assessment_lead = "Fishy McFishface",   notes = "any caveats in this dataset like missing years or changes to assessment models" )

previous_headings: Using marea to develop FSAR plots
title: Fisheries Science Advisory Report (FSAR) four panel plot example in marea
text: Individual plots can easily constructed using marea plotting functions varying style arguments. can use @ operator access ea_data data slot filter data using $ operator. can easily embedded plot function. Since plots ggplot2 objects, extra layers can added customize plots.
code: # Fishing mortality pF <- plot(fsar_example[   fsar_example@data$panel.category == "Fishing" & fsar_example@data$ts.name == "Ut" ], style = "ribbon") +   labs(title = "Fishing Mortality", y = "Mortality (1/yr)", subtitle = "")  # Biomass with additional Reference Points pbiomass <- plot(fsar_example[   fsar_example@data$panel.category == "Biomass" & fsar_example@data$ts.name == "Survey" ], style = "ribbon") +   labs(title = "Biomass", y = "Biomass (kt)", subtitle = "") +   geom_hline(yintercept = 10.5, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +   geom_hline(yintercept = 22, color = "green", linetype = "dashed")  # Catches pcatch <- plot(fsar_example[   fsar_example@data$panel.category == "Catch" & fsar_example@data$ts.name == "CanadaTotal" ], style = "histogram") +   labs(title = "Fish NAFO div", y = "Catch (t)", subtitle = "")  # Recruitment pabun <- plot(fsar_example[   fsar_example@data$panel.category == "Recruitment" & fsar_example@data$ts.name == "RVpred" ], style = "ribbon") +   labs(title = "RV Survey (modeled)", y = "Abundance (millions)", subtitle = "")

title: How to: Update Datasets
text: vignette provides step--step instructions updating datasets included marea package. dataset corresponding R script data-raw/ folder contains code download, process, save data.

title: How to: Update Datasets
text: dataset update scripts follow similar pattern: Load required packages Download access raw data external sources Process clean data Convert ea_data ea_spatial class metadata Save using usethis::use_data()

title: How to: Update Datasets
text: Script location: data-raw/coastwide-indices/coastwide-indices.R Datasets updated: - nao - North Atlantic Oscillation Index - oni - Oceanic Niño Index - pdo - Pacific Decadal Oscillation - soi - Southern Oscillation Index - npgo - North Pacific Gyre Oscillation - mei - Multivariate ENSO Index - ao - Arctic Oscillation Index - amo - Atlantic MultiDecadal Oscillation Index - azmp_bottom_temperature - AZMP Bottom Temperature Update instructions: Install azmpdata package updating AZMP-related data: Run script line--line sections. indices downloaded automatically public NOAA/NCEP servers. index converted ea_data object using make_ea_index() helper function appropriate metadata. script handles temporary file downloads cleanup automatically. Data sources: - NOAA Climate Prediction Center - NOAA NCEI - DFO Atlantic Zone Monitoring Program (azmpdata package)
code: remotes::install_github("casaultb/azmpdata")

title: How to: Update Datasets
text: Script location: data-raw/grey-seals/grey-seals.R Datasets updated: - grey_seals - Grey Seal Abundance estimates Update instructions: Update assess_yr variable current assessment year (line 8). Obtain latest data file assessment contact (Nell den Heyer) place appropriate directory (currently R:/Science/BIODataSvc/SRC/marea/). Update file path filename needed (line 20). Update citation information match latest assessment report. Run script process data save package. Data source: - DFO Seal Assessment (den Heyer et al.) - Bayesian state-space model estimates

title: How to: Update Datasets
text: Script location: data-raw/glorys/glorys-bottom-temperature.R Datasets updated: - glorys_bottom_temperature - Bottom temperature GLORYS reanalysis Update instructions: Create environment variables CMEMS_USERNAME CMEMS_PASSWORD Set Python virtual environment named “CopernicusMarine” copernicusmarine package Update start10 end10 variables desired time period (currently last 10 years) script downloads two datasets: cmems_mod_glo_phy_my_0.083deg_P1M-m cmems_mod_glo_phy_myint_0.083deg_P1M-m Spatial bounds pre-set Maritimes region clean_glorys_nc() function processes netCDF files Data converted sf format cleaned Requirements: - Python environment copernicusmarine package - Valid Copernicus Marine credentials - reticulate, stars, sf R packages

title: How to: Update Datasets
text: Script location: data-raw/eco-indicators/eco-indicators.R Datasets updated: - eco_indicators - Multiple ecological indicators RV surveys Update instructions: Obtain latest CSV files data contact (Jamie C. Tam): eco_indicators_nafo.csv eco_indicators_esswss.csv Place files R:/Science/BIODataSvc/SRC/marea/ update data_dir path. Run script : Reads CSV files Joins together Filters years appropriate (note: 2018 2021 data quality issues) Removes standardized columns (ending “_s”) data converted ea_data object multiple value columns. Data source: - DFO RV Summer Ecosystem Survey - Commercial fisheries landings data - Calculated using marindicators R package Citation: Bundy et al. 2017

title: How to: Update Datasets
text: Script location: data-raw/satellite-ocean-colour/satellite_ocean_colour.R Datasets updated: - t_start - Bloom start day - t_duration - Bloom duration - amplitude_real - Maximum chlorophyll concentration - magnitude_real - Total chlorophyll produced - annual_mean - Average annual chlorophyll - NRMSE_bloom - Root mean square error - percent_dineof - Percentage DINEOF-estimated days Update instructions: script uses get_erddap_data() download CIOOS Atlantic ERDDAP server. Quality control filters applied: Filter data NRMSE_bloom > 0.5 Filter standardized anomalies |z-score| > 4 metric processed separately converted ea_spatial object (spatiotemporal data). script creates multilayer rasters layer represents year. Data source: - SOPhyE group, DFO - CIOOS Atlantic ERDDAP server - OC-CCI satellite ocean colour data

title: How to: Update Datasets
text: Script location: data-raw/coastline/coastline.R Datasets updated: - coastline - Coastline geometry mapping Update instructions: simple script downloads Natural Earth data using rnaturalearth. Extracts coastlines Canada (CA), United States (US), Greenland (GL), Saint Pierre Miquelon (PM). Select relevant columns save. dataset rarely needs updating unless Natural Earth releases new versions. Data source: - Natural Earth Data via rnaturalearth package

title: How to: Update Datasets
text: Always run scripts line--line first check errors understand process. Update citations metadata data sources change new assessments released. Check data quality viewing summaries plotting saving. Document changes script comments, including date, name, reason update. Test updated data running examples documentation (?dataset_name). Version control: Commit changes git clear commit messages.

title: Plotting EA Classes
text: Since plots return ggplot2 objects, can easily customize :
code: # Start with base plot p <- plot(nao, style = "plain")  # Add custom elements p +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +   geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = TRUE, alpha = 0.3) +   labs(     title = "North Atlantic Oscillation Index",     subtitle = "With trend line and zero reference",     caption = "Data source: NOAA"   ) +   theme_minimal()

what: Working with subset of time series data
title: Plotting EA Classes
code: # Start with ea_data object marea::azmp_bottom_temperature # this data contains time series bottom temperature for multiple regions  # filter bottom temperature for one region bottom_temp_cs <- azmp_bottom_temperature[azmp_bottom_temperature@data$region == "Cabot Strait"]  # Add custom elements plot(bottom_temp_cs) +   labs(title = "Bottom Temperature for the Cabot Strait")

title: Plotting EA Classes
text: Compare different indices:
code: # Load multiple climate indices data(nao) data(oni)  # Create comparison plot nao_recent <- nao@data[nao@data$year >= 2000, ] oni_recent <- oni@data[oni@data$year >= 2000 & oni@data$month == 1, ] # January only  # Combine data combined_data <- data.frame(   year = nao_recent$year,   NAO = nao_recent$value,   ONI = oni_recent$value[match(nao_recent$year, oni_recent$year)] )  ea_df <- ea_data(   data = combined_data,   value_col = "NAO",   data_type = "Climate Indices Comparison",   region = "North Atlantic",   location_descriptor = "Global",   units = "Index Value" )  plot(ea_df, style = "default") +   geom_line(aes(y = ONI), color = "blue") +   labs(     title = "Climate Indices: NAO vs ONI",     y = "Index Value",     x = "Year"   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: Plotting EA Classes
text: Create correlation plots:
code: # Example correlation plot (if sufficient data overlap exists) recent_nao <- ea.subset(nao, "year", c(1990:2024))  # Add trend analysis p <- plot(recent_nao, style = "plain") +   labs(x = "Year", y = "NAO Index") +   ggtitle("NAO Index Over Time (1990 - Present)")  # Add regression line lm_fit <- lm(value ~ year, data = recent_nao@data) p <- p +   geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +   theme_minimal()  # Add text with trend slope <- round(coef(lm_fit)[2], 4)  p <- p +   geom_text(     aes(       x = 2000, y = max(recent_nao@data$value),       label = paste("Trend:", slope, "per year")     ),     color = "red", hjust = 0   )  print(p)

title: Plotting EA Classes
text: example shows compare multiple datasets side side using base R graphics. useful visualizing trends across different datasets single view. Compare different datasets side side: can also use patchwork:
code: # Create a multi-panel plot par(mfrow = c(2, 2))  # Panel 1: NAO plot(nao@data$year, nao@data$value,   type = "l",   main = "North Atlantic Oscillation",   xlab = "Year", ylab = "Index" )  # Panel 2: Grey Seals plot(grey_seals@data$year, grey_seals@data$value,   type = "l",   main = "Grey Seal Abundance",   xlab = "Year", ylab = "Count" )  # Panel 3: ONI (subset) oni_subset <- oni@data[oni@data$month == 1, ] # January only plot(oni_subset$year, oni_subset$value,   type = "l",   main = "Oceanic Niño Index (January)",   xlab = "Year", ylab = "Temperature Anomaly (°C)" )  # Panel 4: Bottom Temperature if (exists("azmp_bottom_temperature")) {   data(azmp_bottom_temperature)   bottom_temp_eb <- azmp_bottom_temperature[azmp_bottom_temperature@data$region == "Emerald Basin"]   plot(bottom_temp_eb@data$year, bottom_temp_eb@data$value,     type = "p",     main = "Emerald Basin Bottom Temperature",     xlab = "Year", ylab = "Temperature (°C)"   ) }  # Reset to single panel par(mfrow = c(1, 1)) # Panel 1: NAO p1 <- plot(nao) +   labs(title = "NAO") +   ylab("NAO")  # Panel 2: Grey Seals p2 <- plot(grey_seals) +   labs(title = "Grey seals") +   ylab("abundance")  # Panel 3: ONI (subset) oni_subset <- oni[oni@data$month == 1] # January only p3 <- plot(oni_subset) +   labs(title = "ONI") +   ylab("ONI")  # Panel 4: Bottom Temperature bottom_temp_eb <- azmp_bottom_temperature[azmp_bottom_temperature@data$region == "Emerald Basin"] p4 <- plot(bottom_temp_eb) +   labs(title = "Emerald Basin") +   ylab("bottom temp (degC)")  # Reset to single panel require(patchwork)  (p1 + p2) / (p3 + p4)

title: Plotting EA Classes
text: Apply custom color palettes:
code: # Custom color scheme for temperature data custom_temp_plot <- function(data_obj) {   p <- plot(data_obj, style = "fill")    p +     scale_fill_gradient2(       low = "blue", mid = "white", high = "red",       midpoint = mean(data_obj@data$value, na.rm = TRUE),       name = "Temperature\n(°C)"     ) +     theme_minimal() }  # Apply to spatial data if (exists("glorys_2024")) {   custom_temp_plot(glorys_2024) }

title: Plotting EA Classes
text: Add reference lines annotations:
code: p <- plot(nao, style = "default")  p +   geom_hline(yintercept = c(-1, 1), linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +   annotate("text",     x = 1960, y = 1.2, label = "Positive phase",     hjust = 0, size = 3   ) +   annotate("text",     x = 1960, y = -1.2, label = "Negative phase",     hjust = 0, size = 3   ) +   labs(     title = "North Atlantic Oscillation with Phase Indicators",     caption = "Dashed lines show ±1 standard deviation"   )

title: Plotting EA Classes
text: Display customize uncertainty bounds:
code: # Plot with custom confidence styling p <- plot(grey_seals, style = "default")  p +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), alpha = 0.3, fill = "lightblue") +   geom_line(color = "darkblue") +   geom_point(color = "darkblue") +   labs(     title = "Grey Seal Abundance with 95% Confidence Intervals",     y = "Abundance (thousands)"   ) +   theme_bw()

title: Plotting EA Classes
text: Save plots publications:
code: # Create publication-ready plot pub_plot <- plot(grey_seals, style = "ribbon") +   labs(     title = "Grey Seal Population Trends",     subtitle = "Sable Island, Nova Scotia",     x = "Year",     y = "Estimated Abundance (thousands)",     caption = "Data source: DFO Science"   ) +   theme_bw() +   theme(     plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),     plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12),     axis.title = element_text(size = 12),     axis.text = element_text(size = 10)   )  # Save as high-resolution PNG ggsave("grey_seals_trends.png", pub_plot,   width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 300 )  # Save as PDF for vector graphics ggsave("grey_seals_trends.pdf", pub_plot,   width = 8, height = 6 )

title: Plotting EA Classes
text: Check Data structure first Use Appropriate Styles Time series: Use “default” “ribbon” cases Anomalies: Use “anomaly” style climate indices Biomass: Use “biomass” style population data Spatial: Use “fill” continuous fields, “bubble” point data Customize gradually  Consistent styling
code: # Always inspect your data first ea.print(nao) ea.summary(nao) str(nao@data) # Start simple p1 <- plot(nao)  # Add one element at a time p2 <- p1 + theme_minimal() p3 <- p2 + labs(title = "Custom Title") p4 <- p3 + geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE)  # Final result p4 #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' # Create a custom theme for all your plots my_theme <- theme_bw() +   theme(     plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold", colour = "darkblue"),     axis.title = element_text(size = 12),     legend.position = "bottom"   )  # Apply to any plot plot(nao) + my_theme

title: Understanding Generic EA Data Classes
text: marea package built around robust S4 class objects: - ea_data time series/tabular data - ea_spatial spatio-temporal gridded data makes marea datasets work – easy plotting, inspecting, analysis.

title: Understanding Generic EA Data Classes
text: R “class” defines object behaves. Classes allow marea automatically: - Store rich metadata dataset - Print, summarize, plot datasets using custom methods - Guarantee consistent, predictable structures (collaborators) two core classes : - ea_data: 1D/tabular/time series (e.g. abundances, environmental indices) - ea_spatial: gridded spatial/time-varying fields (e.g. maps temperature)

title: Understanding Generic EA Data Classes
text: tabular time-series data marea use ea_data class.

what: Accessing Data and Metadata: The S4 Structure
title: Understanding Generic EA Data Classes
text: ea_data object, might want extract specific pieces information, like raw data frame single metadata value. ea_data S4 object, two primary ways : standard @ operator direct slot access, custom [[ method flexible convenient access. ea_data object two main “slots”: meta: list holds metadata (e.g., region, units, source_citation). data: tibble contains time series data (year, value, columns).

previous_headings: Understanding Data Classes in marea > Accessing Data and Metadata: The S4 Structure
title: Understanding Generic EA Data Classes
text: @ operator standard way access slots S4 object. can use pull entire meta list data tibble. method direct explicit requires know exact slot names. get single metadata item, need chain operators, like halifax_temp@meta$region.
code: # Use the halifax_temp object created earlier # Access the entire metadata list halifax_temp@meta ## $data_type ## [1] "Bottom Temperature" ##  ## $region ## [1] "Maritimes" ##  ## $location_descriptor ## [1] "Halifax Line Station 2" ##  ## $units ## [1] "°C" ##  ## $species ## [1] NA ##  ## $source_citation ## [1] "DFO Maritimes Region Monitoring Program" ##  ## $original_value_col ## [1] "avg_temp" # Access the entire data tibble halifax_temp@data ## # A tibble: 11 × 3 ##     year avg_temp_value station   ##    <int>          <dbl> <chr>     ##  1  2010            8.2 Halifax-2 ##  2  2011            8.5 Halifax-2 ##  3  2012            8.1 Halifax-2 ##  4  2013            8.9 Halifax-2 ##  5  2014            9.2 Halifax-2 ##  6  2015            8.8 Halifax-2 ##  7  2016            9.1 Halifax-2 ##  8  2017            8.7 Halifax-2 ##  9  2018            9   Halifax-2 ## 10  2019            8.6 Halifax-2 ## 11  2020            9.3 Halifax-2

title: Understanding Generic EA Data Classes
text: make life easier, marea provides custom [[ method. powerful shortcut lets access entire meta data slots, well individual items within , using single, consistent syntax. [[ method first checks asking “meta” “data”. , checks name provided exists meta slot. ’s , finally checks ’s column name ` data slot. approach generally recommended provides single, intuitive interface retrieving almost piece information ea_data object without needing remember underlying @ syntax.
code: # Get the entire meta list (same as @meta) halifax_temp[["meta"]] ## $data_type ## [1] "Bottom Temperature" ##  ## $region ## [1] "Maritimes" ##  ## $location_descriptor ## [1] "Halifax Line Station 2" ##  ## $units ## [1] "°C" ##  ## $species ## [1] NA ##  ## $source_citation ## [1] "DFO Maritimes Region Monitoring Program" ##  ## $original_value_col ## [1] "avg_temp" # Get a specific metadata item halifax_temp[["region"]] ## [1] "Maritimes" # Get the entire data tibble (same as @data) halifax_temp[["data"]] ## # A tibble: 11 × 3 ##     year avg_temp_value station   ##    <int>          <dbl> <chr>     ##  1  2010            8.2 Halifax-2 ##  2  2011            8.5 Halifax-2 ##  3  2012            8.1 Halifax-2 ##  4  2013            8.9 Halifax-2 ##  5  2014            9.2 Halifax-2 ##  6  2015            8.8 Halifax-2 ##  7  2016            9.1 Halifax-2 ##  8  2017            8.7 Halifax-2 ##  9  2018            9   Halifax-2 ## 10  2019            8.6 Halifax-2 ## 11  2020            9.3 Halifax-2 # Get a specific data column (vector) halifax_temp[["year"]] ##  [1] 2010 2011 2012 2013 2014 2015 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020

title: Plot an ea_data object with multiple styles — plot-ea_data
text: Creates time series plot ea_data object. Several plotting styles available, allowing flexible visualization time series data, uncertainty, anomalies.

title: Plot an ea_data object with multiple styles — plot-ea_data
code: # S4 method for class 'ea_data,missing' plot(   x,   style = c("default", "ribbon", "plain", "biomass", "anomaly", "histogram", "indicator",     "indicator_ref", "diversity", "temperature_regime", "nao_enhanced"),   reference_period = c(1991, 2020),   sd_threshold = 1,   highlight_recent = TRUE,   show_trend = TRUE,   regime_threshold = 0.5,   mean_line_color = "red",   trend_line_color = "blue",   warm_color = "red",   cold_color = "blue",   ... )

title: Plot an ea_data object with multiple styles — plot-ea_data
text: x ea_data object. style Character; One : "default", "ribbon", "plain", "biomass", "anomaly", "histogram", "indicator", "indicator_ref", "diversity", "temperature_regime", "nao_enhanced". reference_period Numeric vector length 2. Years defining reference period standardized anomalies. Default c(1991, 2020) climate consistency. Used "indicator_ref" "temperature_regime" styles. sd_threshold Numeric. Number standard deviations threshold lines point classification indicator styles. Default 1 (+/-1 SD). Use 0.5 tighter bounds larger values wider bounds. highlight_recent Logical. Whether highlight recent 5 years. Default TRUE indicator styles. show_trend Logical. Whether add trend line statistics. Default TRUE indicator styles. regime_threshold Numeric. Threshold regime change detection standardized units. Default 0.5 standard deviations. mean_line_color Color mean reference line. Default "red". trend_line_color Color trend line. Default "blue". warm_color Color warm/positive anomalies. Default "red". cold_color Color cold/negative anomalies. Default "blue". ... Additional arguments passed underlying geoms (geom_line, geom_point, geom_ribbon, geom_col, geom_errorbar).

title: Plot an ea_data object with multiple styles — plot-ea_data
text: ggplot object.

title: Plot an ea_data object with multiple styles — plot-ea_data
text: available styles : "default": simple line plot points. "ribbon": line plot points shaded confidence interval ribbon (requires lower upper columns data). "plain": line plot without points embellishments. "biomass": style mimics pacea biomass plots, featuring bold line, points, optional uncertainty ribbon. "anomaly": bar plot positive values colored red negative values blue, suitable anomaly time series. "histogram": simple bar plot showing values year. Creates single-layer plot can easily customized additional geoms like trend lines reference lines. "indicator": Ecosystem indicator style mean reference line trend analysis. Shows long-term mean recent 5-year period highlighting. "indicator_ref": Indicator style 1991-2020 climate reference period. Shows standardized anomalies relative climate normal period. "diversity": Specialized style diversity indices regime change detection period comparisons. "temperature_regime": Temperature anomaly visualization regime shift detection, warm/cold period highlighting, trend analysis. "nao_enhanced": Enhanced NAO visualization phase indicators, regime periods, standardized anomaly coloring.

title: Plot an ea_data object with multiple styles — plot-ea_data
code: # Create sample data with uncertainty df <- data.frame(   year = 2000:2010,   biomass_t = rlnorm(11, meanlog = 5, sdlog = 0.3) ) df$lower <- df$biomass_t * 0.8 df$upper <- df$biomass_t * 1.2  # Create an ea_data object biomass_obj <- ea_data(df,   value_col = "biomass_t",   data_type = "Haddock Biomass",   region = "Georges Bank",   location_descriptor = "5Z",   units = "tonnes" )  # Use different plotting styles plot(biomass_obj, style = "default")  plot(biomass_obj, style = "ribbon")  plot(biomass_obj, style = "biomass")  plot(biomass_obj, style = "histogram")   # Histogram with custom additions plot(biomass_obj, style = "histogram") +   ggplot2::geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red") #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
